mappings.rationale: Directly covers the broad arc of medieval European history, feudalism, kingship, and the church, the core subjects of this entire section.
Directly covers subsections C and F on the early and middle Byzantine Empire including Justinian, Iconoclasm, and the Macedonian era.
Directly covers subsection D2a on Anglo-Saxon England from Alfred the Great through the Danish invasions to the Norman Conquest.
Directly covers subsection D2c on the Viking Age including widespread raids, conquests, social organization, and conversion to Christianity.
Covers subsection D3 on the development of the Holy Roman Empire under the Saxon dynasty and Otto I.
Directly relevant to subsection D2a on the Norman Conquest of England in 1066.
Directly relevant to subsection B on the eclipse of the Western Roman Empire and the transition to Germanic successor states.
Relevant to subsections B2-B3 on the Christianization of Germanic peoples and the role of the church in early medieval society.
Relevant to subsections C2b, E2, and F3 on the Byzantine church, ecclesiastical controversies, and the schism with Rome in 1054.
Relevant to subsection D6 on the development of feudalism and the feudal nobility in early medieval Europe.
Relevant to subsection B2c on the effects of the rise of Islam on western Europe and C3a on Byzantine losses to the Arabs.
Relevant to subsection B3b on arts, intellectual life, and education in the early Middle Ages.
Relevant to subsections B2-B3 on the role of monasticism in the conversion and culture of early medieval Europe.
Relevant to subsection B3b on intellectual life in the early Middle Ages.
Relevant to subsection D2b on the development of the Kingdom of Scotland and its relations with England.
Covers subsection E2c-e on the rise of the Rus, Kievan Rus, and eastern European state formation.
Relevant to the dynastic politics of the Carolingians, Capetians, and other ruling families discussed throughout this section.
Relevant to subsection B1 on the end of the Western Roman Empire and the continuity of Roman institutions.
Relevant to subsection D4 on early Capetian-era political developments and the broader theme of kingship and church-state relations.
